The **liturgical year**, also called the **church year**, **Christian year**, **ecclesiastical calendar**, or **kalendar**,[[1]](#cite_note-MW2021-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) consists of the cycle of [liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") days and seasons that determines when [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day"), including [celebrations of saints](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints "Calendar of saints"), are to be observed, and which portions of scripture are to be read.[[3]](#cite_note-Dowden1910-3)

Distinct [liturgical colours](/wiki/Liturgical_colours "Liturgical colours") may be used in connection with different seasons of the liturgical year. The dates of the festivals vary somewhat among the different churches, although the sequence and logic is largely the same.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Liturgical_year.svg/250px-Liturgical_year.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Liturgical_year.svg)

The liturgical year of some Western churches, indicating the [liturgical colours](/wiki/Liturgical_colours "Liturgical colours")

The liturgical cycle divides the year into a series of seasons, each with their own mood, [theological](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") emphases, and modes of [prayer](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer"), which can be signified by different ways of decorating churches, colours of [paraments](/wiki/Parament "Parament") and [vestments](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment") for clergy, [scriptural](/wiki/Bible "Bible") readings, themes for preaching and even different traditions and practices often observed personally or in the home. In churches that follow the liturgical year, the [scripture passages for each Sunday](/wiki/Lection "Lection") (and even each day of the year in some traditions) are specified in a [lectionary](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary").

After the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), Anglicans and Lutherans continued to follow the lectionary of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"). Following a decision of the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council"), the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") revised that lectionary in 1969, adopting a three-year cycle of readings for Sundays and a two-year cycle for weekdays.

Adaptations of the revised Roman Rite lectionary were adopted by Protestants, leading to the publication in 1994 of the [Revised Common Lectionary](/wiki/Revised_Common_Lectionary "Revised Common Lectionary") for Sundays and major feasts, which is now used by many Protestant denominations, including also [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [United](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches"), some [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches"), etc. This has led to a greater awareness of the traditional Christian year among Protestants, especially among [mainline](/wiki/Mainline_(Protestant) "Mainline (Protestant)") denominations.

## Biblical calendars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Biblical calendars")]

Scholars are not in agreement about whether the calendars used by the Jews before the [Babylonian exile](/wiki/Babylonian_exile "Babylonian exile") were [solar](/wiki/Solar_calendar "Solar calendar") (based on the return of the same relative position between the Sun and the Earth), [lunisolar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") (based on months that corresponded to the cycle of the moon, with periodic additional months to bring the calendar back into agreement with the solar cycle) like the present-day [Jewish calendar](/wiki/Jewish_calendar "Jewish calendar") of [Hillel II](/wiki/Hillel_II "Hillel II"), or [lunar](/wiki/Lunar_calendar "Lunar calendar"), such as the [Hijri calendar](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The first month of the Hebrew year was called אביב (Aviv), meaning the month of green ears of grain. Having to occur at the appropriate time in the spring, it thus was originally part of a [tropical calendar](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year"). At about the time of the [Babylonian exile](/wiki/Babylonian_exile "Babylonian exile"), when using the Babylonian civil calendar, the Jews adopted the term ניסן ([Nisan](/wiki/Nisan "Nisan")) as the name for the month,[[5]](#cite_note-5) based on the Babylonian name Nisanu.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Thomas J Talley says that the adoption of the Babylonian term occurred even before the exile.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the earlier calendar, most of the months were simply called by a number (such as "the fifth month"). The Babylonian-derived names of the month that are used by Jews are:

1. [Nisan](/wiki/Nisan "Nisan") (March–April)
2. [Iyar](/wiki/Iyar "Iyar") (April–May)
3. [Sivan](/wiki/Sivan "Sivan") (May–June)
4. [Tammuz](/wiki/Tammuz_(Hebrew_month) "Tammuz (Hebrew month)") (June–July)
5. [Av](/wiki/Av_(month) "Av (month)") (July–August)
6. [Elul](/wiki/Elul "Elul") (August–September)
7. [Tishrei](/wiki/Tishrei "Tishrei") (September–October)
8. [Marcheshvan](/wiki/Marcheshvan "Marcheshvan") (October–November)
9. [Kislev](/wiki/Kislev "Kislev") (November–December)
10. [Tevet](/wiki/Tevet "Tevet") (December–January)
11. [Shevat](/wiki/Shevat "Shevat") (January–February)
12. [Adar 1](/wiki/Adar_1 "Adar 1") (February; only during leap years)
13. [Adar](/wiki/Adar "Adar") (February–March)

In Biblical times, the following Jewish religious feasts were celebrated:

* [Pesach](/wiki/Pesach "Pesach") (Passover) – 14 [Nisan](/wiki/Nisan "Nisan") (sacrifice of a lamb), 15 Nisan ([Passover seder](/wiki/Passover_seder "Passover seder"))
* [Chag HaMatzot](/wiki/Chag_HaMatzot "Chag HaMatzot") (Unleavened Bread) – 15–21 Nisan
* [Reishit Katzir](/wiki/Reishit_Katzir "Reishit Katzir") (Firstfruits) – 16 Nisan
* [Shavuot](/wiki/Shavuot "Shavuot") (Weeks) – Fiftieth day counted from Passover, normally 6–7 [Sivan](/wiki/Sivan "Sivan")
* [Rosh Hashanah](/wiki/Rosh_Hashanah "Rosh Hashanah") (Trumpets) – 1–2 [Tishrei](/wiki/Tishrei "Tishrei")
* [Yom Kippur](/wiki/Yom_Kippur "Yom Kippur") (Atonement) – 10 Tishrei
* [Sukkot](/wiki/Sukkot "Sukkot") (Tabernacles) – 15–21 Tishrei
* [Chanukah](/wiki/Chanukah "Chanukah") (Dedication) – 25 [Kislev](/wiki/Kislev "Kislev")–2/3 [Tevet](/wiki/Tevet "Tevet") (instituted in 164 BC)
* [Purim](/wiki/Purim "Purim") (Lots) – 14–15 [Adar](/wiki/Adar "Adar") (instituted in c. 400 BC)

## Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

### East Syriac Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: East Syriac Rite")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/East_syrian_Liturgical_calender.png/250px-East_syrian_Liturgical_calender.png)](/wiki/File:East_syrian_Liturgical_calender.png)

The liturgical seasons of [East Syriac Catholic churches](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite")

Further information: [Liturgical calendar of the Syro-Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar_of_the_Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Church "Liturgical calendar of the Syro-Malabar Catholic Church")

The Liturgical Calendar of the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") is fixed according to the flow of salvation history. With a focus upon the historical life of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ"), believers are led to the eschatological fulfillment (i.e. the heavenly bliss) through this special arrangement of liturgical seasons.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The liturgical year is divided into 8 seasons of approximately 7 weeks each but adjusted to fit the solar calendar. The arrangement of the Seasons in the Liturgical Year is based on seven central events on celebrations of the Salvation History. They are:

1. [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") of Christ
2. [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") of Christ
3. [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") of Christ
4. [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")
5. [Transfiguration](/wiki/Transfiguration_of_Jesus "Transfiguration of Jesus")
6. [Glorious Cross](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Cross "Feast of the Cross")
7. [Parousia](/wiki/Parousia "Parousia") (the Dedication of Church after Christ's second coming)

One of the oldest available records mentioning the liturgical cycle of east-syriac rite is handwritten manuscript named 'Preface to Hudra' written by Rabban Brick-Iso in 14th century.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The manuscript mentions that the liturgical year is divided into nine seasons starting from Subara and ends with Qudas Edta. Catholic churches of east-syriac rite maintains the same liturgical calendar until the current date except that many consider 7th and 8th seasons as a single one. The biblical reading and prayers during Mass and [Liturgy of the Hours](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours") vary according to different seasons in the liturgical calendar.

#### Liturgical Calendar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Liturgical Calendar")]

The various seasons of the liturgical calendar of [Syro-Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church") and [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church") are given below.

##### Annunciation (Subara)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Annunciation (Subara)")]

Weeks of Annunciation ([Subara](/wiki/Subara "Subara")) is the first season of the liturgical year. The liturgical year begins with the commemoration of biblical events leading to the annunciation and birth of Jesus as expected savior in the old testament. The season begins on the Sunday just before the first of December and ends with the feast of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") that is [the Feast of the Baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord"). The faithful practice abstinence during December 1–25 in preparation for Christmas; this period is called "25 days Lent".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Feasts celebrated during this season

* [Feast of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Feast of the Immaculate Conception") of [Mary, mother of Jesus](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") (December 8)
* Feast of Miraculous Cross of Mylapore ([Saint Thomas Christian cross](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christian_cross "Saint Thomas Christian cross")) (December 18) in [Syro Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro_Malabar_Church "Syro Malabar Church")
* [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") of Our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ or [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") (December 25)
* Feast of [Holy Infants](/wiki/Massacre_of_the_Innocents "Massacre of the Innocents") (December 28)
* Feast of [Name Iso](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple") (January 1)
* Feast of [Mary, mother of Jesus](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") (last Friday of Season)

##### Epiphany (Denha)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Epiphany (Denha)")]

Weeks of Epiphany begins on the Sunday closest to the feast of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") and runs to the beginning of Great Fast. The word *denha* in [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") means sunrise. Church considers the baptism of Jesus in the [River Jordan](/wiki/River_Jordan "River Jordan") as the first historical event in which the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") was revealed to humankind in the person of Jesus Christ. Thus the season commemorates the manifestation or revelation of Jesus and Trinity to the world. During the season the church celebrates the feasts of Saints in connection with the manifestation of the Lord.

Feasts celebrated during the period

* Feast of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") or [Feast of Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Baptism_of_the_Lord "Baptism of the Lord") (January 6)
* Feast of [Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist") on first Friday of Epiphany
* Feast of Apostles [Peter (Kepha)](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") and [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") on second Friday of Epiphany
* Feast of [Evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists") on third Friday of Epiphany
* Feast of [Saint Stephan](/wiki/Saint_Stephen "Saint Stephen") on fourth Friday of Epiphany
* Feast of [Fathers of Church](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") on fifth Friday of Epiphany
* Feast of [Patron Saint of Church](/wiki/Patron_Saint "Patron Saint") on sixth Friday of Epiphany
* Feast of [all departed faithful](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day") on last Friday of Epiphany

##### Great Fast (Sawma Rabba)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Great Fast (Sawma Rabba)")]

During these weeks the faithful meditate over the 40-day fast of Jesus and the culmination of his public life in passion, death and burial. The season begins 50 days before Easter on Peturta Sunday and comprises the whole period of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") and culminates on [Resurrection Sunday](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). Word Peturta in Syriac means "looking back" or "reconciliation".
Faithful enter the weeks of Great Fast, celebrating the memory of all the Faithful Departed on the last Friday of Denha.

According to the ecclesial tradition, the weeks of Great Fast is also an occasion to keep up the memory of the beloved Departed through special prayers, renunciation, almsgiving, and so on and thus prepare oneself for a good death and resurrection in Jesus Christ. During the fast faithful of [Syro Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro_Malabar_Church "Syro Malabar Church") do not use meat, fish, egg, many dairy products, and most favorite food items, and avoid sexual contacts on all days including Sundays and Feast days. Before European colonization, [Indian Nasranis](/wiki/Nasrani_(India) "Nasrani (India)") used to have food only once a day (after 3:00 pm) on all days during Great Fast.
-
Feasts in the Lenten Season

* Peturta Sunday on First Sunday of Great Fast
* [Ash Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") or [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") on the first day (Monday) of Great Fast
* [Lazarus](/wiki/Lazarus_of_Bethany "Lazarus of Bethany") Friday on the sixth Friday of Great Fast
* [Oshana Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") on the seventh Sunday of Great Fast
* [Thursday of Pesha](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday")
* [Friday of Passion](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") or [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday")
* [Great Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") or Saturday of Light

The following feasts are always in the Lenten Season:

* Feast of Mar [Cyril of Jerusalem](/wiki/Cyril_of_Jerusalem "Cyril of Jerusalem") (March 18)
* Feast of [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") (March 19)
* [Feast of the Annunciation](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation "Feast of the Annunciation") (March 25)

##### Resurrection (Qyamta)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Resurrection (Qyamta)")]

The weeks of Great Resurrection begin on the [Resurrection Sunday](/wiki/Easter "Easter") and run to the feast of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"). The Church celebrates the Resurrection of our Lord during these seven weeks: Jesus' victory over death, sin, suffering and Satan. The church also commemorates various events that occurred after the resurrection of Christ, such as the visits of Jesus to the Apostles and the ascension of Jesus.

According to eastern Christianity, the Feast of Resurrection is the most important and the greatest feast in a liturgical year. Therefore, the season commemorating the resurrection of Christ is also of prime importance in the church liturgy. The first week of the season is celebrated as the 'Week of weeks' as it is the week of the resurrection of Christ.

Feasts celebrated during the period:

* Feast of [Resurrection of Christ](/wiki/Easter "Easter")
* Feast of All Confessors (Saints) on the first Friday of [Qyamta](/wiki/Qyamta "Qyamta")
* [New Sunday](/wiki/Quasimodo_Sunday "Quasimodo Sunday") or St. Thomas Sunday on the second Sunday of Qyamta
* Feast of [Ascension of Jesus](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") on the sixth Friday of Qyamta

The following feasts are always in the season of resurrection:

* Feast of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") (April 24)
* Feast of [Mark the Evangelist](/wiki/Mark_the_Evangelist "Mark the Evangelist") (April 25)
* Feast of [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph"), the worker (May 1)
* Feasts of [Saint Philip](/wiki/Philip_the_Apostle "Philip the Apostle") and [Saint James](/wiki/James,_son_of_Alphaeus "James, son of Alphaeus") the apostles (May 3)

##### Apostles (Slihe)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Apostles (Slihe)")]

Weeks of apostles ([Slihe](/wiki/Slihe "Slihe")) starts on the feast of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), fiftieth day of the [Resurrection Sunday](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). During these days the church commemorates the inauguration of church and the acts of apostles and church fathers through which the foundation of the church was laid. Church meditates on the virtues of the early church: fellowship, breaking of bread and sharing of wealth, and the [fruits](/wiki/Fruit_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Fruit of the Holy Spirit") and [gifts](/wiki/Seven_gifts_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Seven gifts of the Holy Spirit") of [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"). The spread of the church all over the world as well as her growth is also remembered during this season.

Feast celebrated during the season:

* Feast of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") on first Sunday of Slihe
* Feast of Friday of Gold: The first commemoration of the first miracle of apostles done by [Saint Peter](/wiki/Peter_the_Apostle "Peter the Apostle").

The following feasts are commemorated in the season of Slihe

* Feast of [Mar Aphrem](/wiki/Ephrem_the_Syrian "Ephrem the Syrian") (June 10)
* Feast of the Apostles [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") and [Paul](/wiki/Saint_Paul "Saint Paul") (June 29)
* Feast of [Mar Thoma](/wiki/Thomas_the_Apostle "Thomas the Apostle"), founding father of east Syriac churches (July 3)
* Feast of [Mar Quriaqos and Yolitha](/wiki/Cyricus_and_Julitta "Cyricus and Julitta") (July 15)

##### Qaita (Summer)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Qaita (Summer)")]

Along the weeks of Qaita maturity and fruitfulness of church are commemorated. The Syriac word Qaita means "summer" and it is a time of harvest for the Church. The fruits of the Church are those of holiness and martyrdom. While the sprouting and infancy of the Church were celebrated in 'the Weeks of the Apostles,' her development in different parts of the world by reflecting the image of the heavenly Kingdom and giving birth to many saints and martyrs are proclaimed during this season. Fridays of this Season are set apart for honoring saints and martyrs.

Feast celebrated during the season:

* Feast of the [twelve apostles](/wiki/Twelve_apostles "Twelve apostles") and Nusardeil on the first Sunday of Qaita (Nusardeil is a Persian word which means "God-given New Year Day").
* Feast of [Mar Jacob of Nisibis](/wiki/Jacob_of_Nisibis "Jacob of Nisibis") on the first Friday of Qaita.
* Feast of [Mar Mari](/wiki/Saint_Mari "Saint Mari") on the second Friday of Qaita.
* Feast of [Marta Simoni and her Seven Children](/wiki/Woman_with_seven_sons "Woman with seven sons") on the fifth Friday of Qaita.
* Feast of [Mar Shimun Bar Sabbai](/wiki/Shemon_Bar_Sabbae "Shemon Bar Sabbae") and Companions on the sixth Friday of Qaita.
* Feast of martyr Mar Quardag on the seventh Friday of Qaita.

The following feasts are commemorated in the season of Qaita

* Feast of seventy disciples of Jesus (July 27)
* Feast of [Saint Alphonsa](/wiki/Saint_Alphonsa "Saint Alphonsa") in [Syro Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro_Malabar_Catholic_Church "Syro Malabar Catholic Church") (July 28)
* Feast of [Transfiguration of Jesus](/wiki/Transfiguration_of_Jesus "Transfiguration of Jesus") (August 6)
* Feast of [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") (August 15)

##### Eliyah-Sliba-Moses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Eliyah-Sliba-Moses")]

The name of the seasons of Eliyah-Sliba-Moses takes their origin from the feast of the [transfiguration of Jesus](/wiki/Transfiguration_of_Jesus "Transfiguration of Jesus"). And the seasons revolve around the exaltation of the cross on the [feast of the glorious cross](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Cross "Feast of the Cross") on September 14. During the seasons of Eliyah and Sliba church reminds the faithful of the heavenly bliss which is promised to be inherited at the end of earthly life and the church commemorates the exaltic experience of the bliss through various sacraments. While during the season of Moses church meditates upon the end of time and the [last judgment](/wiki/Last_judgment "Last judgment"). Many at times the season of Moses is regarded as a distinct and separate season from the other two since it has a distinct theme.

The season of Eliyah has a length of one to three Sundays. Season of Sliba starts on Sunday on or after the feast of the glorious cross and has a length of three to four weeks. The first Sunday of Sliba is always considered as the fourth Sunday of the combined season. The season of Moses always has four weeks.

Feast celebrated during the seasons:

* [Feast of the glorious Cross](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Cross "Feast of the Cross")

The following feasts are commemorated in the seasons of Eliyah-Sliba-Moses

* Feast of [Nativity of Mary](/wiki/Nativity_of_Mary "Nativity of Mary") on September 8 and the eight-day fast in preparation for the feast

##### Dedication of the church (Qudas Edta)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Dedication of the church (Qudas Edta)")]

The weeks of the dedication of the church is the last liturgical season in the East Syriac rite.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) It consists of four weeks and ends on the Saturday before Sunday between November 27 and December 3. The theme of the season is that the church is presented by Christ as his eternal bride before his father at the heavenly bride chamber. The period has its origin in the feast of the dedication of the church of Sephelcure or the Jewish feast of Hanukkah. However, the season was officially instituted by Patriarch Isho-Yahb III of Seleucia-Ctesiphon (647–657) by separating it from the season of Moses.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Feasts celebrated during the season:

* Feast of dedication of the church on 1st Sunday of Qudas Edta
* [Feast of Christ the King](/wiki/Feast_of_Christ_the_King "Feast of Christ the King") on last Sunday of Qudas Edta (Celebrated only in eastern catholic churches of the rite since pope [Pius XI](/wiki/Pius_XI "Pius XI") instituted it in Roman-rite).

### Eastern Orthodox Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox Church")]

Main article: [Eastern Orthodox liturgical calendar](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_liturgical_calendar "Eastern Orthodox liturgical calendar")

The liturgical year in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") is characterized by alternating [fasts](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") and [feasts](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints "Calendar of saints"), and is in many ways similar to the Catholic year. However, Church New Year ([Indiction](/wiki/Indiction "Indiction")) traditionally begins on [September 1](/wiki/September_1_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "September 1 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)") ([Old Style or New Style](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates")), rather than the first Sunday of Advent. It includes both feasts on the [Fixed Cycle](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church_calendar "Eastern Orthodox Church calendar") and the [Paschal Cycle](/wiki/Paschal_Cycle "Paschal Cycle") (or Moveable Cycle). The most important feast day by far is the Feast of [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter") (Easter) – the Feast of Feasts. Then the [Twelve Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts "Great Feasts"), which commemorate various significant events in the lives of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") and of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") ([Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary")).

The majority of Orthodox Christians (Russians, in particular) follow the [Julian Calendar](/wiki/Julian_Calendar "Julian Calendar") in calculating their ecclesiastical feasts, but many (including the Ecumenical Patriarchate and the Church of Greece), while preserving the Julian calculation for feasts on the Paschal Cycle, have adopted the [Revised Julian Calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_Calendar "Revised Julian Calendar") (at present coinciding with the [Gregorian Calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_Calendar "Gregorian Calendar")) to calculate those feasts which are fixed according to the calendar date.

Between 1900 and 2100, there is a thirteen-day difference between the dates of the Julian and the Revised Julian and Gregorian calendars. Thus, for example, where Christmas is celebrated on December 25 O.S. ([Old Style](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates")), the celebration coincides with [January 7](/wiki/December_25_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "December 25 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)") in the Revised Calendar. The computation of the day of [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter") (Easter) is, however, always computed according to a [lunar calendar](/wiki/Lunar_calendar "Lunar calendar") based on the Julian Calendar, even by those churches which observe the Revised Calendar.

There are four fasting seasons during the year: The most important fast is [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") which is an intense time of fasting, [almsgiving](/wiki/Almsgiving "Almsgiving") and prayer, extending for forty days prior to [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") and [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), as a preparation for [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). The [Nativity Fast](/wiki/Nativity_Fast "Nativity Fast") (Winter Lent) is a time of preparation for the Feast of the [Nativity of Christ](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") (Christmas), but whereas Advent in the West lasts only four weeks, Nativity Fast lasts a full forty days. The [Apostles' Fast](/wiki/Apostles%27_Fast "Apostles' Fast") is variable in length, lasting anywhere from eight days to six weeks, in preparation for the [Feast of Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Feast_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul "Feast of Saints Peter and Paul") ([June 29](/wiki/June_29_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "June 29 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)")). The [Dormition Fast](/wiki/Dormition_Fast "Dormition Fast") lasts for two weeks from [August 1](/wiki/August_1_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "August 1 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)") to [August 14](/wiki/August_14_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "August 14 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)") in preparation for the Feast of the [Dormition of the Theotokos](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Theotokos "Dormition of the Theotokos") ([August 15](/wiki/August_15_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "August 15 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)")). The liturgical year is so constructed that during each of these fasting seasons, one of the Great Feasts occurs, so that fasting may be tempered with joy.

In addition to these fasting seasons, Orthodox Christians fast on Wednesdays and Fridays throughout the year (and some Orthodox [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") also observe Monday as a fast day). Certain fixed days are always fast days, even if they fall on a Saturday or Sunday (in which case the fast is lessened somewhat, but not abrogated altogether); these are: The [Decollation](/wiki/Decollation "Decollation") of St. [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), the [Exaltation of the Cross](/wiki/Exaltation_of_the_Cross "Exaltation of the Cross") and the day before the Epiphany (January 5). There are several fast-free periods, when it is forbidden to fast, even on Wednesday and Friday. These are: the week following Pascha, the week following [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), the period from the Nativity of Christ until January the 5th and the first week of the [Triodion](/wiki/Triodion "Triodion") (the week following the 17th Sunday before Pentecost).

#### Pascha

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pascha")]

Main article: [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter")

The greatest feast is Pascha. Easter for both East and West is calculated as the first Sunday after the [full moon](/wiki/Full_moon "Full moon") that falls on or after March 21 (nominally the day of the [vernal equinox](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox")), but the Orthodox calculations are based on the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), whose March 21 corresponds at present with April 3 of the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"), and on calculations of the date of full moon different from those used in the West (see [computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus") for further details).

The date of Pascha is central to the entire ecclesiastical year, determining not only the date for the beginning of Great Lent and Pentecost, but affecting the cycle of [moveable feasts](/wiki/Moveable_feast "Moveable feast"), of scriptural readings and the [Octoechos](/wiki/Octoechos_(liturgy) "Octoechos (liturgy)") (texts chanted according to the eight ecclesiastical modes) throughout the year. There are also a number of lesser feasts throughout the year that are based upon the date of Pascha. The moveable cycle begins on the [Zacchaeus](/wiki/Zacchaeus "Zacchaeus") Sunday (the first Sunday in preparation for Great Lent or the 33rd Sunday after Pentecost as it is known), though the cycle of the Octoechos continues until Palm Sunday.

The date of Pascha affects the following liturgical seasons:

* The period of the [Triodion](/wiki/Triodion "Triodion") (the Sundays before Great Lent, [Cheesefare Week](/wiki/Cheesefare_Week "Cheesefare Week"), Palm Sunday, and Holy Week)
* The period of the [Pentecostarion](/wiki/Pentecostarion "Pentecostarion") (Sunday of Pascha through the Sunday After Pentecost which is also called the Sunday of all saints)

#### The twelve Great Feasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The twelve Great Feasts")]

Main article: [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts "Great Feasts")

Some of these feasts follow the Fixed Cycle, and some follow the Moveable (Paschal) Cycle. Most of those on the Fixed Cycle have a period of preparation called a [Forefeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast"), and a period of celebration afterward, similar to the Western [Octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)"), called an Afterfeast. Great Feasts on the Paschal Cycle do not have Forefeasts. The lengths of Forefeasts and Afterfeasts vary, according to the feast.

* **[Nativity of the Theotokos](/wiki/Nativity_of_the_Theotokos "Nativity of the Theotokos")** ([September 8](/wiki/September_8_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "September 8 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + birth of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") to [Joachim](/wiki/Joachim "Joachim") and [Anna](/wiki/Saint_Anne "Saint Anne")
* **[Elevation of the Cross](/wiki/Exaltation_of_the_Cross "Exaltation of the Cross")** ([September 14](/wiki/September_14_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "September 14 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + the rediscovery of the original [Cross](/wiki/Christian_Cross "Christian Cross") on which Christ was crucified
* **[Entrance of the Theotokos into the Temple](/wiki/Presentation_of_Mary "Presentation of Mary")** ([November 21](/wiki/November_21_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "November 21 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + the entry of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") into the Temple around the age of 3
* **[Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") of Our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ** ([December 25](/wiki/December_25_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "December 25 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + the birth of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"), or [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")
* **[Theophany](/wiki/Theophany "Theophany")** ([January 6](/wiki/January_6_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "January 6 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + the baptism of Jesus Christ, Christ's blessing of the water, and the revealing of Christ as God
* **[Presentation of Our Lord in the Temple](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple")** ([February 2](/wiki/February_2_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "February 2 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + Christ's presentation as an infant in the Temple by the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") and [Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph").
* **[Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos")** ([March 25](/wiki/March_25_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "March 25 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + [Gabriel](/wiki/Archangel_Gabriel "Archangel Gabriel")'s announcement to the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") that she will conceive the Christ, and her wilful agreement thereto

> Note: In Eastern practice, should this feast fall during Holy Week or on Pascha itself, the feast of the Annunciation is not transferred to another day. In fact, the conjunction of the feasts of the Annunciation and Pascha (*dipli Paschalia*, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): διπλή Πασχαλιά) is considered an extremely festive event.

* **[Entry into Jerusalem](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday")** (Sunday before [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter"))
  + known in the West as Palm Sunday.
* **[Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")** (40 days after Pascha)
  + Christ's ascension into Heaven following his resurrection.
* **[Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")** (50 days after Pascha)
  + The [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") comes and indwells the [apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles") and other Christian believers.
* **[Transfiguration of Our Lord](/wiki/Transfiguration_of_Jesus "Transfiguration of Jesus")** ([August 6](/wiki/August_6_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "August 6 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + Christ's Transfiguration as witnessed by Peter, James and John.
* **[Dormition of the Theotokos](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Theotokos "Dormition of the Theotokos")** ([August 15](/wiki/August_15_(Eastern_Orthodox_liturgics) "August 15 (Eastern Orthodox liturgics)"))
  + The falling asleep of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") (cf. the [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"))

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other feasts")]

Some additional feasts are observed as though they were Great Feasts:

* [The Protection of the Mother of God](/wiki/The_Protection_of_the_Mother_of_God "The Protection of the Mother of God") (October 1), especially among the [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox "Russian Orthodox")
* The Feast of [Saint James the Just](/wiki/Saint_James_the_Just "Saint James the Just") (October 23)

* The Feast of [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas"), the Bishop of [Myra](/wiki/Myra "Myra") in [Lycia](/wiki/Lycia "Lycia") (December 6)
* The Feast of the Conception of [Mary](/wiki/Saint_Mary "Saint Mary") by Saints [Joachim](/wiki/Joachim "Joachim") and [Anne](/wiki/Saint_Anne "Saint Anne") (December 9)
* The Feast of [Saint Spiridon](/wiki/Saint_Spiridon "Saint Spiridon") (December 12)
* The Feast of [Saint Stephen](/wiki/Saint_Stephen "Saint Stephen") the Deacon (December 27)
* The Feast of [Saint Basil the Great](/wiki/Saint_Basil_the_Great "Saint Basil the Great") and the [Circumcision of Christ](/wiki/Circumcision_of_Christ "Circumcision of Christ") (January 1)
* The Feast of the [Three Holy Hierarchs](/wiki/Three_Holy_Hierarchs "Three Holy Hierarchs"): [Basil the Great](/wiki/Basil_the_Great "Basil the Great"), [Gregory the Theologian](/wiki/Gregory_the_Theologian "Gregory the Theologian") and [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom") (January 30)
* The Feast of the [Forty Martyrs of Sebaste](/wiki/Forty_Martyrs_of_Sebaste "Forty Martyrs of Sebaste") (March 9)
* The Feast of [Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick "Saint Patrick") (March 17)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* The Feast of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") (April 23)
* The Feast of the Holy Emperors [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") and [Helen](/wiki/Helena_(Empress) "Helena (Empress)") (May 21)
* The [Nativity of Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Nativity_of_Saint_John_the_Baptist "Nativity of Saint John the Baptist") (June 24)
* The Feast of [Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Saints_Peter_and_Paul "Saints Peter and Paul") (June 29)
* The Feast of [Saint Elijah](/wiki/Saint_Elijah "Saint Elijah") the [Prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet") (July 20)
* The Feast of [Saint Christina of Bolsena](/wiki/Saint_Christina_of_Bolsena "Saint Christina of Bolsena") the Great Martyr (July 24)
* The [Beheading of St. John the Baptist](/wiki/Beheading_of_St._John_the_Baptist "Beheading of St. John the Baptist") (August 29)
* Beginning of the [Indiction](/wiki/Indiction "Indiction")-Ecclesiastical Year (September 1)
* The [Patronal Feast](/wiki/Patron_Saint "Patron Saint") of a church or monastery

Every day throughout the year commemorates some saint or some event in the lives of [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") or the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"). When a feast on the moveable cycle occurs, the feast on the fixed cycle that was set for that calendar day is transferred, with the [propers](/wiki/Proper_(liturgy) "Proper (liturgy)") of the feast often being chanted at [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline") on the nearest convenient day.

## Western Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Western Christianity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/BritLibCottonCleoBIXLiturgicalCalOct.jpg/250px-BritLibCottonCleoBIXLiturgicalCalOct.jpg)](/wiki/File:BritLibCottonCleoBIXLiturgicalCalOct.jpg)

The month of October from a liturgical calendar for [Abbotsbury Abbey](/wiki/Abbotsbury_Abbey "Abbotsbury Abbey"). 13th-century manuscript ([British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library"), [Cotton MS Cleopatra](/wiki/Cotton_MS_Cleopatra "Cotton MS Cleopatra") B IX, folio 59r).

See also: [Movable Western Christian observances](/wiki/Movable_Western_Christian_observances "Movable Western Christian observances")

Western Christian liturgical calendars are based on the cycle of the Roman Rite of the Catholic Church, and are also followed in many Protestant churches, including the Lutheran, Anglican, and other traditions. Generally, the seasons in liturgical western Christianity are [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"), [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), Ordinary Time (Time after [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)")), [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), and Ordinary Time (Time after [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")). Some Protestant traditions do not include Ordinary Time: every day falls into a denominated season. Other Protestant churches, such as a minority in the Reformed tradition, reject the liturgical year entirely on the grounds that its observance is not directed in scripture.[[15]](#cite_note-Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church-15)

For those that follow the Western liturgical year, the [Revised Common Lectionary](/wiki/Revised_Common_Lectionary "Revised Common Lectionary") provides scriptural structure for the patterns of the seasons. Protestant denominations that follow this lectionary include Methodists, Anglicans/Episcopalians, Lutherans, Presbyterians, some Baptists and Anabaptists, among others.[[16]](#cite_note-16) With regard to the calendars of the Western Christian Churches that use the Revised Common Lectionary,[[17]](#cite_note-Hickman2010-17) [Vanderbilt University](/wiki/Vanderbilt_University "Vanderbilt University") Professor Hoyt L. Hickman, states that:[[17]](#cite_note-Hickman2010-17)

> All these calendars agree that the Lord's Day is of primary importance among the observances of the year and that the Christian Year contains two central cycles – the Easter cycle and the Christmas cycle. Each cycle includes a festival season (Easter and Christmas), preceded by a season of preparation and anticipation (Lent and Advent). In most denominational versions and in the *Common Lectionary*, Lent and Advent are immediately preceded by a transitional Sunday (Transfiguration and Christ the King), and the Easter and Christmas Seasons are immediately followed by a transitional Sunday (Trinity and Baptism of the Lord).[[17]](#cite_note-Hickman2010-17)

Protestant Churches, with exception of the Lutheran and Anglican, generally observe fewer if any feasts with regard to the saints than the aforementioned liturgical denominations, in addition to the Catholic and Orthodox Churches.

### Denominational specifics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Denominational specifics")]

#### Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

Main article: [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar "General Roman Calendar")

In summary, the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") "unfolds within the cycle of a year ... the whole mystery of Christ, from the incarnation and birth until the ascension, the day of Pentecost, and the expectation of blessed hope and of the coming of the Lord. Recalling thus the mysteries of redemption, the Church opens to the faithful the riches of her Lord's powers and merits, so that these are in some way made present for all time, and the faithful are enabled to lay hold upon them and become filled with saving grace." Within this cycle, the resurrection of Jesus is celebrated both weekly, on Sundays, and annually, at Easter, "together with His blessed passion".[[18]](#cite_note-v2-18): Para. 102 A number of changes to the Catholic Church's liturgical year were put in place by the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council").[[18]](#cite_note-v2-18): para. 107

Various other events in the life of Christ and his saints are also recalled and celebrated on specific days or seasons of each year.

1. In its [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") the liturgical year begins with [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"), the time of preparation for both the [nativity of Christ](/wiki/Nativity_of_Christ "Nativity of Christ"), and his expected second coming at the end of time.[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19) The Advent season lasts until the first [vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") of [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") on December 24.
2. [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") follows, beginning with First Vespers of Christmas on the evening of December 24 and ending with the [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord"),[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19) on the first Sunday after [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") (the latter is on January 6 generally).[[20]](#cite_note-20)
3. A period of [ordinary time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") includes the days between Christmastide and the start of Lent.[[21]](#cite_note-:1-21)
4. [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") is the period of purification and penance that begins on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") and ends on [Holy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday").[[22]](#cite_note-OneFaith116-22)
5. The [Mass of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Lord%27s_Supper "Mass of the Lord's Supper") on the evening of Holy Thursday marks the beginning of the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum"), which includes [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday"), and [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday").[[22]](#cite_note-OneFaith116-22) The days of the Easter Triduum recall Christ's last supper with his disciples, his capture and passion, his death on the cross, burial, and resurrection.[[22]](#cite_note-OneFaith116-22)
6. The seven-week liturgical [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") immediately follows the Triduum, climaxing at [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"). This last feast recalls the descent of the Holy Spirit upon Jesus' disciples after the [Ascension of Jesus](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus").[[22]](#cite_note-OneFaith116-22)
7. The remaining period of ordinary time includes the period between Eastertide and the start of Advent.[[21]](#cite_note-:1-21)

There are many forms of liturgy in the Catholic Church. Even putting aside the many Eastern rites in use, the [Latin liturgical rites](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites") alone include the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite"), the [Mozarabic Rite](/wiki/Mozarabic_Rite "Mozarabic Rite"), and the [Cistercian Rite](/wiki/Cistercian_Rite "Cistercian Rite"), as well as other forms that have been largely abandoned in favour of adopting the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"). There are also historical versions of the liturgy that varied greatly from the present one, such those used by the [Anglo-Saxon Church](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Church "Anglo-Saxon Church").

The liturgical calendar in that form of the Roman Rite (see [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar "General Roman Calendar")) [of 1960](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_1960 "General Roman Calendar of 1960") differs in some respects from that of the present form of the Roman Rite.

#### Anglican Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Anglican Church")]

Further information: [List of Anglican Church Calendars](/wiki/List_of_Anglican_Church_Calendars "List of Anglican Church Calendars")

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), [Mother Church](/wiki/Mother_Church "Mother Church") of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), uses a liturgical year that is in most respects identical to that of the 1969 Catholic Common Lectionary. While the calendars contained within the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* and the *[Alternative Service Book](/wiki/Alternative_Service_Book "Alternative Service Book")* (1980) have no "Ordinary Time", *[Common Worship](/wiki/Common_Worship "Common Worship")* (2000) adopted the ecumenical 1983 [Revised Common Lectionary](/wiki/Revised_Common_Lectionary "Revised Common Lectionary"). The few exceptions are Sundays following Christmas and [Transfiguration](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Transfiguration "Feast of the Transfiguration"), observed on the last Sunday before Lent instead of on *[Reminiscere](/wiki/Reminiscere "Reminiscere")*.

In some Anglican traditions (including the Church of England) the Christmas season is followed by an Epiphany season, which begins on the Eve of the Epiphany (on January 6 or the Sunday after January 1) and ends on the [Feast of the Presentation](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple") (on February 2 or the Sunday after January 27). Ordinary Time begins after this period. Traditionally, within Anglicanism, Sundays are counted after Trinity rather than after Pentecost, and the season is called “Trinitytide” rather than “Ordinary Time”.

The *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* contains within it the traditional Western Eucharistic lectionary which traces its roots to the *Comes* of St. Jerome in the 5th century.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Its similarity to the ancient lectionary is particularly obvious during Trinity season (Sundays after the Sunday after Pentecost), reflecting that understanding of sanctification.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

#### Reformed Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Reformed Churches")]

[Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") Christians emphasize weekly celebration of the [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day"). While some of them celebrate also what they call the five [evangelical feasts](/wiki/Evangelical_feast "Evangelical feast"), others celebrate no holy days but the Lord's Day and reject the liturgical year as non-scriptural, and as therefore inconsistent with the [regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship").[[15]](#cite_note-Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church-15)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Liturgical calendar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Liturgical calendar")]

#### Advent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Advent")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Rok_liturgiczny_-_Liturgical_year.jpg/250px-Rok_liturgiczny_-_Liturgical_year.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rok_liturgiczny_-_Liturgical_year.jpg)

Roman Rite liturgical year

Main article: [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent")

[Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") (from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *[adventus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Adventus "wiktionary:Adventus")*, which means "arrival" or "coming") is the first season of the liturgical year. It begins four Sundays before Christmas, the Sunday falling on or nearest to November 30, and ends on Christmas Eve. Traditionally observed as a "fast", it focuses on preparation for the coming of [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ"), not only the coming of the Christ-child at Christmas, but also, in the first weeks, on the [eschatological](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology") final coming of Christ, making Advent "a period for devout and joyful expectation".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

This season is often marked by the [Advent Wreath](/wiki/Advent_Wreath "Advent Wreath"), a garland of evergreens with four candles. Although the main symbolism of the advent wreath is simply marking the progression of time, many churches attach themes to each candle, most often 'hope', 'faith', 'joy', and 'love'. Other popular devotions during Advent include the use of the [Advent Calendar](/wiki/Advent_Calendar "Advent Calendar") or the [Tree of Jesse](/wiki/Tree_of_Jesse "Tree of Jesse") to count down the days to Christmas.

*Liturgical colour*: violet or purple;[[27]](#cite_note-27) blue in some traditions, such as Anglican/Episcopalian, Methodist, and Lutheran.[[28]](#cite_note-UMCblue-28)[[29]](#cite_note-TECblue-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### Christmastide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Christmastide")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Advent_Wreath_on_Christmas_Eve_%28Broadway_United_Methodist_Church%29.jpg/250px-Advent_Wreath_on_Christmas_Eve_%28Broadway_United_Methodist_Church%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Advent_Wreath_on_Christmas_Eve_(Broadway_United_Methodist_Church).jpg)

A white coloured [parament](/wiki/Parament "Parament") hangs from the [pulpit](/wiki/Pulpit "Pulpit"), indicating that the current liturgical season is [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide"). The fact that the Christ Candle in the centre of the [Advent wreath](/wiki/Advent_wreath "Advent wreath") is lit also indicates that [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") has arrived.

Main article: [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide")

The [Christmas season](/wiki/Christmas_season "Christmas season") immediately follows Advent. The traditional [Twelve Days of Christmas](/wiki/Twelve_Days_of_Christmas "Twelve Days of Christmas") begin with [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") on the evening of [December 24](/wiki/December_24 "December 24") and continue until the feast of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"). The actual Christmas season continues until the [Feast of the Baptism of Christ](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_Christ "Feast of the Baptism of Christ"), which is celebrated on the Sunday after January 6, or the following Monday if that Sunday is kept as Epiphany.[[31]](#cite_note-maternalheart.org-31)

In the pre-1970 form, this feast is celebrated on January 13, unless January 13 is a Sunday, in which case the feast of the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family") is celebrated instead.[[31]](#cite_note-maternalheart.org-31) Until the suppression of the Octave of the Epiphany in the 1960 reforms, January 13 was the Octave day of the Epiphany, providing the date for the end of the season.

Traditionally, the end of Christmastide was February 2, or the [Feast of the Presentation](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple") of the Lord, also known as [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas"). This feast recounts the 40 days of rest Mary took before being purified and presenting her first-born son to the Temple in Jerusalem. In medieval times, Candlemas eve (Feb. 1st) marked the day when all Christmas decorations, including the [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") and the [Nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene"), were taken down. However, the tradition of ending Christmastide on Candlemas has slowly waned, except in some pockets of the Hispanic world where Candlemas (or La Fiesta de la Candelaria) is still an important feast and the unofficial end of the Christmas season.

*Liturgical colour*: white

#### Ordinary Time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Ordinary Time")]

Main article: [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time")

"Ordinary" comes from the same root as our word "ordinal", and in this sense means "the counted weeks". In the Catholic Church and in some Protestant traditions, these are the common weeks which do not belong to a proper season. In Latin, these seasons are called the weeks *per annum*, or "through the year".

In the current form of the Roman Rite adopted following the Second Vatican Council, Ordinary Time consists of 33 or 34 Sundays and is divided into two sections. The first portion extends from the day following the Feast of the Baptism of Christ until the day before Ash Wednesday (the beginning of Lent). It contains anywhere from three to eight Sundays, depending on how early or late Easter falls.

The main focus in the readings of the Mass is Christ's earthly ministry, rather than any one particular event. The counting of the Sundays resumes following Eastertide; however, two Sundays are replaced by Pentecost and Trinity Sunday, and depending on whether the year has 52 or 53 weeks, one may be omitted.

In the pre-1970 form of the Roman Rite, the Time after Epiphany has anywhere from one to six Sundays. As in the current form of the rite, the season mainly concerns Christ's preaching and ministry, with many of his parables read as the Gospel readings. The season begins on January 14[[32]](#cite_note-32) and ends on the Saturday before Septuagesima Sunday. Omitted Sundays after Epiphany are transferred to Time after Pentecost and celebrated between the Twenty-Third and the Last Sunday after Pentecost according to an order indicated in the [Code of Rubrics](/wiki/Code_of_Rubrics "Code of Rubrics"), 18, with complete omission of any for which there is no Sunday available in the current year.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Before the 1960 revisions, the omitted Sunday would be celebrated on the Saturday before Septuagesima Sunday,[[34]](#cite_note-34) or, in the case of the Twenty-Third Sunday after Pentecost, on the Saturday before the Last Sunday after Pentecost.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

*Liturgical colour*: green

#### Pre-Lent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Pre-Lent")]

Main article: [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent")

[Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great") is the first to document a period of preparation for Easter beginning with [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima"), whose name refers to a period of around seventy days before Easter.[[36]](#cite_note-Crampton-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) This pre-Lenten period lasts two and a half weeks, encompassing [Sexagesima](/wiki/Sexagesima "Sexagesima") and [Quinquagesima](/wiki/Quinquagesima "Quinquagesima"). It concludes with [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") and [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday").

This period opens an educational period leading up to the reception of [catechumens](/wiki/Catechumens "Catechumens") at Easter.[[38]](#cite_note-Chavesse-38) Events such as [mystery plays](/wiki/Mystery_plays "Mystery plays") from the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") performed during this period historically supported this instructional campaign,[[39]](#cite_note-39) reflecting the traditional [lectionary](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary") for the [Canonical hours](/wiki/Canonical_hours "Canonical hours"), which begins on [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima") with the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis"), as is still reflected in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The pre-Lenten liturgy introduces some customs of Lent, including the suppression of the [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") and its replacement at Mass with the [Tract](/wiki/Tract_(liturgy) "Tract (liturgy)"). The Gloria is no longer said on Sundays.[[41]](#cite_note-Mahrt-41)

The 1969 reform of the Roman Rite subsumed these weeks liturgically into Ordinary Time,[[42]](#cite_note-Pristas-42) but Carnival is still widely celebrated. A pre-Lenten provision continues in many Anglican and Lutheran liturgies.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

*Liturgical colour* (where observed): violet or purple

#### Lent and Passiontide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Lent and Passiontide")]

Main articles: [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), [Passiontide](/wiki/Passiontide "Passiontide"), and [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week")

Lent is a major penitential season of preparation for [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). It begins on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") and, if the penitential days of [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") and [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") are included, lasts for forty days, since the six Sundays within the season are not counted.

In the Roman Rite, the [Gloria in Excelsis Deo](/wiki/Gloria_in_Excelsis_Deo "Gloria in Excelsis Deo") and the [Te Deum](/wiki/Te_Deum "Te Deum") are not used in the Mass and Liturgy of the Hours respectively, except on Solemnities and Feasts, and the [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") and verse that usually precede the reading of the Gospel is either omitted or replaced with another acclamation.

Lutheran churches make these same omissions.

As in Advent, the deacon and subdeacon of the pre-1970 form of the Roman Rite do not wear their habitual dalmatic and tunicle (signs of joy) in Masses of the season during Lent; instead they wear "folded chasubles", in accordance with the ancient custom.

In the pre-1970 form of the Roman Rite, the two weeks before Easter form the season of Passiontide, a subsection of the Lenten season that begins with [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") of [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") and ends immediately before the Mass of the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil").[[44]](#cite_note-44) In this form, what used to be officially called [Passion Sunday](/wiki/Passion_Sunday "Passion Sunday"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) has the official name of the First Sunday in Passiontide,[[46]](#cite_note-46) and [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") has the additional name of the Second Sunday in Passiontide.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In Sunday and ferial Masses (but not on feasts celebrated in the first of these two weeks) the [Gloria Patri](/wiki/Gloria_Patri "Gloria Patri") is omitted at the [Entrance Antiphon](/wiki/Introit "Introit")[[48]](#cite_note-48) and at the Lavabo,[[49]](#cite_note-49) as well as in the responds in the Divine Office.

In the post-1969 form of the Roman Rite, "Passion Sunday" and "Palm Sunday" are both names for the Sunday before Easter, officially called "Palm Sunday of the Lord's Passion". The former Passion Sunday became a fifth Sunday of Lent. The earlier form reads Matthew's account on Sunday, Mark's on Tuesday, and Luke's on Wednesday, while the post-1969 form reads the Passion only on Palm Sunday (with the three Synoptic Gospels arranged in a three-year cycle) and on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), when it reads the Passion according to John, as also do earlier forms of the Roman Rite.

The veiling of crucifixes and images of the saints with violet cloth, which was obligatory before 1970, is left to the decision of the national bishops' conferences. In the United States, it is permitted but not required, at the discretion of the pastor.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In all forms, the readings concern the events leading up to the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") and the betrayal, Passion, and death of Christ.

The week before Easter is called [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").

In the Roman Rite, feasts that fall within that week are simply omitted, unless they have the rank of Solemnity, in which case they are transferred to another date. The only solemnities inscribed in the General Calendar that can fall within that week are those of [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") and the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation").

*Liturgical colour*: violet or purple. The colour rose may be used, where it is the practice, on [Laetare Sunday](/wiki/Laetare_Sunday "Laetare Sunday") (4th Sunday of Lent). On Palm Sunday the colour since 1970 is red, by earlier rules violet or purple, with red being used after 1955 for the blessing of the palms.

#### Easter Triduum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Easter Triduum")]

Main article: [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum")

The Easter Triduum consists of Good Friday, Holy Saturday and Easter Sunday.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Each of these days begins liturgically not with the morning but with the preceding evening.

The triduum begins on the evening before Good Friday with [Mass of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Lord%27s_Supper "Mass of the Lord's Supper"), celebrated with white vestments,[[52]](#cite_note-52) and often includes a ritual of ceremonial footwashing. It is customary on this night for a vigil involving private prayer to take place, beginning after the evening service and continuing until midnight. This vigil is occasionally renewed at dawn, continuing until the Good Friday liturgy.

During the day of [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") Mass is not celebrated in the Catholic Church. Instead a Celebration of the Passion of the Lord is held in the afternoon or evening. It consists of three parts: a [Liturgy of the Word](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Word "Liturgy of the Word") that includes the reading of the account of the [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") by [John the Evangelist](/wiki/John_the_Evangelist "John the Evangelist") and concludes with a solemn [Universal Prayer](/wiki/General_Intercessions "General Intercessions"). Other churches also have their Good Friday commemoration of the Passion.

The colour of vestments varies: no colour, red, or black are used in different traditions. Coloured hangings may be removed. Lutheran churches often either remove colourful adornments and icons, or veil them with drab cloth. The service is usually plain with somber music, ending with the congregation leaving in silence. In the Catholic, some Lutheran, and High Anglican rites, a crucifix (not necessarily the one which stands on or near the altar on other days of the year) is ceremoniously unveiled. Other crucifixes are unveiled, without ceremony, after the service.

[Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") commemorates the day during which Christ lay in the tomb. In the Catholic Church, there is no Mass on this day; the Easter Vigil Mass, which, though celebrated properly at the following midnight, is often celebrated in the evening, is an Easter Mass. With no liturgical celebration, there is no question of a liturgical colour.

The [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") is held in the night between Holy Saturday and Easter Sunday, to celebrate the [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection "Resurrection") of Jesus. See also [Paschal candle](/wiki/Paschal_candle "Paschal candle"). The liturgical colour is white, often together with gold. In the Roman Rite, during the "Gloria in Excelsis Deo" the organ and bells are used in the liturgy for the first time in two days, and the statues, which have been veiled during Passiontide (at least in the Roman Rite through the 1962 version), are unveiled. In Lutheran churches, colours and icons are re-displayed as well.

#### Eastertide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Eastertide")]

Main article: [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide")

[Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") is the celebration of [Jesus' Resurrection](/wiki/Death_and_resurrection_of_Jesus "Death and resurrection of Jesus"). The date of Easter varies from year to year, according to a lunar-calendar dating system (see [computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus") for details). In the Roman Rite, the Easter season extends from the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") through [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") Sunday. In the pre-1970 form of the rite, this season includes also the [Octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)") of Pentecost, so Eastertide lasts until [None](/wiki/None_(liturgy) "None (liturgy)") of the following Saturday.

In the Roman Rite, the [Easter octave](/wiki/Easter_octave "Easter octave") allows no other feasts to be celebrated or commemorated during it; a solemnity, such as the Annunciation, falling within it is transferred to the following Monday. If Easter Sunday or Easter Monday falls on April 25, the Greater Litanies, which in the pre-1970 form of the Roman Rite are on that day, are transferred to the following Tuesday.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

By a decree of May 5, 2000, the Second Sunday of Easter (the Sunday after Easter Day itself), is known also in the Roman Rite as the [Feast of the Divine Mercy](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Divine_Mercy "Feast of the Divine Mercy").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus_Christ "Ascension of Jesus Christ") Thursday, which celebrates the return of Jesus to heaven following his resurrection, is the fortieth day of Easter, but, in places where it is not observed as a [Holy Day of Obligation](/wiki/Holy_Day_of_Obligation "Holy Day of Obligation"), the post-1969 form of the Roman rite transfers it to the following Sunday.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

[Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") is the fiftieth and last day of the Easter season. It celebrates the sending of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") to the [Apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament"), which traditionally marks the birth of the Church, see also [Apostolic Age](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age").

*Liturgical colour*: white, but red on the feast of Pentecost.

#### Ordinary Time, Time after Pentecost, Time after Trinity, or Kingdomtide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Ordinary Time, Time after Pentecost, Time after Trinity, or Kingdomtide")]

Main articles: [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time"), [Kingdomtide](/wiki/Kingdomtide "Kingdomtide"), and [Pentecost season](/wiki/Pentecost_season "Pentecost season")

This season, under various names, follows the Easter season and the feasts of Easter, Ascension, and Pentecost. In the post-1969 form of the Roman rite, Ordinary Time resumes on [Pentecost Monday](/wiki/Pentecost_Monday "Pentecost Monday"), omitting the Sunday which would have fallen on Pentecost. In the earlier form, where Pentecost is celebrated with an octave, the Time after Pentecost begins at Vespers on the Saturday after Pentecost. The Sundays resume their numbering at the point that will make the Sunday before Advent the thirty-fourth, omitting any weeks for which there is no room (present-day form of the Roman Rite) or are numbered as "Sundays after Pentecost" (pre-1970 Roman Rite, [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and some Protestants) or as "Sundays after Trinity" (some Protestants). This season ends on the Saturday before the First Sunday of Advent.

Feasts during this season include:

* [Trinity Sunday](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday"), the first Sunday after Pentecost
* [Solemnity of the Most Holy Body and Blood of Christ](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)") (Roman Rite and some Anglican and Lutheran traditions), Thursday of the second week after Pentecost, often celebrated on the following Sunday
* [Solemnity of the Most Sacred Heart of Jesus](/wiki/Sacred_Heart "Sacred Heart") (Roman Rite), Friday of the third week after Pentecost
* [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") on August 15
* [Feast of Creation](/wiki/Feast_of_Creation "Feast of Creation") on September 1
* [Feast of Christ the King](/wiki/Feast_of_Christ_the_King "Feast of Christ the King"), last Sunday before Advent (Roman Rite, Lutherans, Anglicans) or last Sunday in October (1925–1969 form of the Roman Rite)

In the final few weeks of Ordinary Time, many churches direct attention to the coming of the Kingdom of God, thus ending the liturgical year with an [eschatological](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology") theme that is one of the predominant themes of the season of Advent that began the liturgical year. For instance, in the [extraordinary form of the Roman Rite](/wiki/Tridentine_Mass "Tridentine Mass"), the Gospel of the Last Sunday is [Matthew 24:15–35](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%2024:15–35&version=nrsv) and in the ordinary form of the Roman Rite all the last three Sundays of the liturgical year are affected by the theme of the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming").

While the Roman Rite adopts no special designation for this final part of Ordinary Time, some denominations do, and may also change the liturgical colour. The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") uses the term "Sundays before Advent" for the final four Sundays and permits red vestments as an alternative. The [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") may use the name "[Kingdomtide](/wiki/Kingdomtide "Kingdomtide")".[[56]](#cite_note-BoW-56) The [Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod") (LCMS) uses the terms "Third-Last, Second-Last and Last Sunday in the Church Year" and does not change from green. The LCMS does not officially celebrate a "Feast of Christ the King". The [Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Wisconsin_Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod") (WELS) uses the term "Period of End Times" and assigns red vestments to the first and second Sundays.

##### Hierarchy of feast days

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Hierarchy of feast days")]

Main articles: [Ranking of liturgical days in the Roman Rite](/wiki/Ranking_of_liturgical_days_in_the_Roman_Rite "Ranking of liturgical days in the Roman Rite") and [Principal Holy Day (Anglicanism)](/wiki/Principal_Holy_Day_(Anglicanism) "Principal Holy Day (Anglicanism)")

There are degrees of solemnity of the office of the feast days of saints. In the 13th century, the Roman Rite distinguished three ranks: simple, semidouble and double, with consequent differences in the recitation of the [Divine Office or Breviary](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours"). The simple feast commenced with the chapter (*capitulum*) of First Vespers, and ended with None. It had three lessons and took the psalms of Matins from the ferial office; the rest of the office was like the semidouble. The semidouble feast had two Vespers, nine lessons in Matins, and ended with Compline. The antiphons before the psalms were only intoned.

In the Mass, the semidouble had always at least three "orationes" or [collects](/wiki/Collect "Collect"). On a double feast the antiphons were sung in their entirety, before and after the psalms, while in Lauds and Vespers there were no *suffragia* of the saints, and the Mass had only one "oratio" (if no commemoration was prescribed). If ordinary double feasts (referred to also as lesser doubles) occurred with feasts of a higher rank, they could be simplified, except the octave days of some feasts and the feasts of the Doctors of the Church, which were transferred.

To the existing distinction between major and ordinary or minor doubles, [Pope Clement VIII](/wiki/Pope_Clement_VIII "Pope Clement VIII") added two more ranks, those of first-class or second-class doubles. Some of these two classes were kept with octaves. This was still the situation when the 1907 article [Ecclesiastical Feasts](http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/06021b.htm) in the [Catholic Encyclopedia](/wiki/Catholic_Encyclopedia "Catholic Encyclopedia") was written. In accordance with the rules then in force, feast days of any form of double, if impeded by "occurrence" (falling on the same day)[[58]](#cite_note-58) with a feast day of higher class, were transferred to another day.

[Pope Pius X](/wiki/Pope_Pius_X "Pope Pius X") simplified matters considerably in his 1911 [reform of the Roman Breviary](/wiki/Reform_of_the_Roman_Breviary_by_Pope_Pius_X "Reform of the Roman Breviary by Pope Pius X"). In the case of occurrence the lower-ranking feast day could become a [commemoration](/wiki/Commemoration_(liturgy) "Commemoration (liturgy)") within the celebration of the higher-ranking one. Until then, ordinary doubles took precedence over most of the semidouble Sundays, resulting in many of the Sunday Masses rarely being said. While retaining the semidouble rite for Sundays, Pius X's reform permitted only the most important feast days to be celebrated on Sunday, although commemorations were still made until [Pope John XXIII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXIII "Pope John XXIII")'s reform of 1960.

The division into doubles (of various kinds) semidoubles and simples continued until 1955, when [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") abolished the rank of semidouble, making all the previous semidoubles simples, and reducing the previous simples to a mere commemoration in the Mass of another feast day or of the feria on which they fell (see [General Roman Calendar of Pope Pius XII](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_Pope_Pius_XII "General Roman Calendar of Pope Pius XII")).

Then, in 1960, Pope John XXIII issued the [Code of Rubrics](/wiki/Code_of_Rubrics "Code of Rubrics"), completely ending the ranking of feast days by doubles etc., and replacing it by a ranking, applied not only to feast days but to all liturgical days, as I, II, III, and IV class days.

The [1969 revision](/wiki/Mysterii_Paschalis "Mysterii Paschalis") by [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") divided feast days into "solemnities", "feasts" and "memorials", corresponding approximately to Pope John XXIII's I, II and III class feast days. Commemorations were abolished. While some of the memorials are considered obligatory, others are optional, permitting a choice on some days between two or three memorials, or between one or more memorials and the celebration of the feria. On a day to which no obligatory celebration is assigned, the Mass may be of any saint mentioned in the [Roman Martyrology](/wiki/Roman_Martyrology "Roman Martyrology") for that day.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

##### Assumption of Mary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Assumption of Mary")]

Main article: [Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary")

Observed by Catholics and some Anglicans on August 15, which is the same as the Eastern and Orthodox feast of the [Dormition](/wiki/Dormition "Dormition"), the end of the earthly life of the Virgin Mary and, for some, her bodily Assumption into heaven, is celebrated. The teaching on this [dogma](/wiki/Dogma_in_the_Catholic_Church "Dogma in the Catholic Church") was summed by [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") in his [bull](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") *[Munificentissimus Deus](/wiki/Munificentissimus_Deus "Munificentissimus Deus")* of 1 November 1950.

In other Anglican and Lutheran traditions, as well as a few others, August 15 is celebrated as St. Mary, Mother of the Lord.

*Liturgical colour*: white

## Secular observance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_year&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Secular observance")]

Because of the dominance of Christianity in Europe throughout the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), many features of the Christian year became incorporated into the secular calendar. Many of its feasts (e.g., [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras"), [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day")) remain holidays, and are now celebrated by people of all faiths and none—in some cases worldwide. The secular celebrations bear varying degrees of likeness to the religious feasts from which they derived, often also including elements of ritual from [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") festivals of similar date.
